headline: How Amazon places EC2 instances for its own Black Friday infrastructure
why: On Black Friday, Amazon.com handles millions of orders per second. A single server failure at peak load would cost millions in lost sales. EC2 Placement Groups and instance lifecycle management are critical tools.

topics.usage: Amazon's real-time inventory and pricing engines use Cluster Placement Groups — all instances in the same rack, ultra-low latency between nodes. Prices update across all servers in under 1ms.
The checkout and payment services use Spread Placement Groups — each instance on a different physical rack. If one rack loses power, only 1 out of 7 checkout servers goes down. Orders keep flowing.

During off-peak hours (2am–6am), Amazon stops non-critical batch processing instances (analytics, recommendation model training). This saves cost. They restart automatically before peak traffic begins.

Spot Instances run background ML training jobs. When AWS reclaims a Spot instance with a 2-minute warning, Amazon's system checkpoints the training job and relaunches it on a new instance — stateless by design.

diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph CPG["Cluster Placement Group — Low Latency"]
        I1[Inventory Server 1] --- I2[Inventory Server 2]
        I2 --- I3[Inventory Server 3]
    end
    subgraph SPG["Spread Placement Group — High Availability"]
        R1[Rack 1: Checkout A]
        R2[Rack 2: Checkout B]
        R3[Rack 3: Checkout C]
    end
    User([🛒 Customer]) --> R1
    User --> R2
    User --> R3
    R1 --> CPG
    style CPG fill:#1e3a5f,color:#dbeafe
    style SPG fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
    style User fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
takeaway: Cluster = speed (low latency between nodes). Spread = resilience (no single point of failure). Amazon uses both simultaneously — the right placement group for the right job.
